title: CPR (Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation)
summary: CPR keeps blood and oxygen flowing when the heart has stopped. Begin immediately — every minute without CPR reduces survival by 10%.
steps: Step 1: Ensure the scene is safe. Check for hazards before approaching.
Step 2: Check responsiveness — tap shoulders firmly and shout 'Are you okay?'
Step 3: Call 112 / 108 immediately or ask a bystander to call while you begin CPR.
Step 4: Lay the person on their back on a firm, flat surface.
Step 5: Tilt the head back gently and lift the chin to open the airway.
Step 6: Check for breathing — look, listen, feel for no more than 10 seconds.
Step 7 (ADULT): Place heel of one hand on center of chest (lower half of breastbone). Place other hand on top, interlace fingers.
Step 8: Keep arms straight. Press down hard and fast — at least 5 cm (2 inches) deep at 100–120 compressions per minute (to the beat of 'Stayin' Alive').
Step 9: Allow full chest recoil between compressions. Do not lean on the chest.
Step 10: After 30 compressions, give 2 rescue breaths (if trained). Pinch nose, seal mouth, breathe in for 1 second, watch chest rise.
Step 11: Continue 30:2 ratio until emergency services arrive, the person shows signs of life, or you are physically unable to continue.
Step 12 (CHILD 1–8 years): Use one or two hands. Compress 5 cm deep at 100–120/min. Give 2 gentle rescue breaths after 30 compressions.
Step 13 (INFANT under 1 year): Use 2 fingers on center of chest. Compress 4 cm deep. Give 2 gentle puffs of air after 30 compressions.
warnings: Do NOT delay CPR to look for an AED — start compressions immediately.
Do NOT stop CPR unless the person recovers, emergency services take over, or you are exhausted.
Do NOT perform CPR on a person who is breathing normally.
Do NOT tilt the head if spinal injury is suspected — use jaw-thrust maneuver instead.
when_to_call_emergency: Call 112 / 108 immediately when a person is unresponsive and not breathing normally.

title: Bleeding Control
summary: Uncontrolled bleeding can cause death within minutes. Direct pressure is the most effective first aid for most wounds.
steps: Step 1: Ensure scene safety. Wear gloves if available to protect yourself.
Step 2: Call 112 / 108 / 100 for severe or arterial bleeding (bright red, spurting).
Step 3: Apply firm, direct pressure to the wound using a clean cloth, gauze, or clothing.
Step 4: Press hard and continuously — do not lift the cloth to check. If it soaks through, add more material on top.
Step 5: Maintain pressure for at least 10–15 minutes without releasing.
Step 6: If bleeding is on a limb and is life-threatening, apply a tourniquet 5–7 cm above the wound (not on a joint).
Step 7 (Tourniquet): Wrap the tourniquet twice around the limb. Tighten until bleeding stops. Note the time applied — write it on the person's skin.
Step 8: Elevate the injured limb above heart level if no fracture is suspected.
Step 9: Keep the person warm and lying down to prevent shock.
Step 10: Do not remove embedded objects — apply pressure around them.
Step 11: For internal bleeding (bruising, rigid abdomen, shock signs) — call 112 immediately, keep person still and warm.
warnings: Do NOT remove a tourniquet once applied — only medical professionals should remove it.
Do NOT use a tourniquet on the neck, chest, or abdomen.
Do NOT probe or clean deep wounds — apply pressure and wait for help.
Do NOT give food or water to a person with severe bleeding.
when_to_call_emergency: Call 112 for any wound that won't stop bleeding after 10 minutes of pressure, arterial bleeding, or suspected internal bleeding.

title: Fractures & Broken Bones
summary: Immobilize fractures to prevent further injury. Never attempt to straighten a broken bone.
steps: Step 1: Call 112 for open fractures (bone visible), fractures with severe bleeding, or suspected spinal/pelvic fractures.
Step 2: Do not attempt to straighten or realign the broken bone.
Step 3: Immobilize the fracture in the position found — use a splint, rolled newspaper, or firm padding.
Step 4: Extend the splint beyond the joints above and below the fracture.
Step 5: Secure the splint with bandages, cloth strips, or tape — firm but not tight enough to cut circulation.
Step 6: Check circulation below the fracture — feel for pulse, check skin color and warmth.
Step 7: Apply ice wrapped in cloth to reduce swelling — 20 minutes on, 20 minutes off.
Step 8: Elevate the injured limb if possible and if it doesn't cause more pain.
Step 9: For suspected rib fractures — support the arm on the injured side in a sling. Do not bind the chest tightly.
Step 10: Monitor for shock — pale skin, rapid breathing, confusion. Keep person warm and calm.
warnings: Do NOT move a person with suspected spinal, neck, or pelvic fracture unless in immediate danger.
Do NOT attempt to push a protruding bone back in.
Do NOT apply a splint so tightly it cuts off circulation.
Do NOT give aspirin — it increases bleeding risk.
when_to_call_emergency: Call 112 for open fractures, suspected spinal injury, fractures with severe bleeding, or if the person cannot be safely moved.
video_search_term: fracture splinting first aid Red Cross

title: Burns
summary: Cool the burn immediately with cool running water. Never use ice, butter, or toothpaste.
steps: Step 1: Remove the person from the source of the burn. Ensure scene safety.
Step 2: Call 112 for burns covering large areas, burns on face/hands/genitals/joints, chemical or electrical burns, or burns in children.
Step 3: Cool the burn immediately with cool (not cold) running water for at least 20 minutes.
Step 4: Remove clothing and jewelry near the burn — unless stuck to the skin.
Step 5: Do not break blisters — they protect against infection.
Step 6: Cover the burn loosely with a clean, non-fluffy material (cling film, clean plastic bag, or sterile dressing).
Step 7: Keep the person warm — burns cause rapid heat loss and shock.
Step 8: Elevate burned limbs above heart level to reduce swelling.
Step 9: For chemical burns — brush off dry chemicals first, then flush with large amounts of water for 20+ minutes.
Step 10: For electrical burns — do not touch the person until power is off. Call 112 immediately — internal injuries may not be visible.
warnings: Do NOT use ice, iced water, butter, oil, toothpaste, or any cream on burns.
Do NOT remove clothing stuck to burned skin.
Do NOT break blisters.
Do NOT cover burns with fluffy cotton wool — fibers stick to wounds.
when_to_call_emergency: Call 112 for burns larger than the person's palm, burns on face/hands/feet/genitals, all chemical or electrical burns, and burns in children or elderly.

title: Shock
summary: Shock is a life-threatening condition where the body's organs don't receive enough blood. Act immediately.
steps: Step 1: Call 112 immediately — shock is a medical emergency.
Step 2: Lay the person down on their back on a flat surface.
Step 3: Raise the legs 20–30 cm (8–12 inches) above heart level — unless head, neck, spine, or leg injury is suspected.
Step 4: Do not raise legs if the person has a head injury, breathing difficulty, or broken leg.
Step 5: Keep the person warm — cover with a blanket or coat. Prevent heat loss from the ground.
Step 6: Loosen tight clothing around neck, chest, and waist.
Step 7: Do not give food or water — the person may need surgery.
Step 8: Reassure the person — keep them calm and still.
Step 9: Monitor breathing and pulse every 2 minutes.
Step 10: If the person becomes unconscious and stops breathing, begin CPR.
Step 11: Control any visible bleeding with direct pressure.
Step 12: Do not leave the person alone.
warnings: Do NOT give food, water, or alcohol.
Do NOT apply a heat source directly to the skin.
Do NOT leave the person alone.
Do NOT raise legs if spinal injury, head injury, or broken leg is suspected.
when_to_call_emergency: Call 112 immediately when shock is suspected — pale/cold/clammy skin, rapid weak pulse, rapid breathing, confusion, or loss of consciousness.
video_search_term: treating shock first aid St John Ambulance

title: Unconscious Person
summary: An unconscious person who is breathing should be placed in the recovery position to keep the airway open.
steps: Step 1: Check for danger — ensure the scene is safe.
Step 2: Check responsiveness — tap shoulders and shout 'Are you okay?'
Step 3: Call 112 immediately.
Step 4: Open the airway — tilt head back, lift chin.
Step 5: Check for breathing — look, listen, feel for up to 10 seconds.
Step 6: If NOT breathing — begin CPR immediately (see CPR guide).
Step 7: If BREATHING — place in recovery position:
a) Kneel beside the person. Place the arm nearest you at a right angle to the body.
b) Bring the far arm across the chest, hold the back of the hand against the near cheek.
c) With your other hand, pull up the far knee so the foot is flat on the floor.
d) Keeping the hand against the cheek, pull on the bent knee to roll the person toward you.
e) Tilt the head back to keep the airway open. Adjust the top knee so the hip and knee are at right angles.
Step 8: Monitor breathing continuously until emergency services arrive.
Step 9: If breathing stops at any point, roll onto back and begin CPR.
Step 10: Do not give anything by mouth.
Step 11: Keep the person warm with a blanket.
warnings: Do NOT leave an unconscious person alone.
Do NOT give food or water.
Do NOT move the person if spinal injury is suspected — only move if airway cannot be maintained.
Do NOT put anything in the mouth.
when_to_call_emergency: Call 112 immediately for any unconscious person.
video_search_term: recovery position unconscious person St John Ambulance

title: Road Accident Response
summary: A systematic approach to road accident scenes — protect, alert, locate, manage. Safety first, always.
steps: Step 1 — PROTECT: Switch on hazard lights. Park safely off the road. Wear a high-visibility vest if available.
Step 2 — PROTECT: Place warning triangles 45m behind the accident on a normal road, 150m on a motorway.
Step 3 — ALERT: Call 112 / 108 / 100. State: your location (road name, landmarks, GPS coordinates), number of vehicles, number of casualties, types of injuries.
Step 4 — LOCATE: Approach the scene carefully. Watch for fuel leaks, fire, unstable vehicles.
Step 5: Turn off vehicle ignitions if safe to do so — reduces fire risk.
Step 6: Do not move casualties unless there is immediate danger (fire, flooding).
Step 7: Check each casualty — are they conscious? Breathing?
Step 8: For unconscious casualties — open airway, check breathing. Begin CPR if not breathing.
Step 9: For conscious casualties — reassure them, keep them still, control bleeding.
Step 10: Do not remove helmets from motorcyclists unless airway is blocked and you cannot maintain it otherwise.
Step 11: Keep bystanders back — maintain a clear area for emergency vehicles.
Step 12: Stay on the line with emergency services — follow their instructions.
Step 13: When emergency services arrive — give a clear handover: number of casualties, injuries found, treatment given.
warnings: Do NOT move casualties with suspected spinal injuries.
Do NOT smoke near the accident scene — fuel leaks are common.
Do NOT remove a motorcyclist's helmet unless absolutely necessary for airway management.
Do NOT block the road with your vehicle — pull well off to the side.
when_to_call_emergency: Call 112 immediately for any road accident with injuries, unconscious persons, or trapped casualties.
video_search_term: road accident first aid response ROSPA

title: Choking (Heimlich Maneuver)
summary: Choking is a life-threatening emergency. Act immediately — a person can lose consciousness within minutes.
steps: Step 1: Ask 'Are you choking?' If the person can cough, speak, or breathe — encourage them to keep coughing.
Step 2: If they cannot cough, speak, or breathe — call 112 and begin treatment immediately.
Step 3 — BACK BLOWS: Stand to the side and slightly behind. Support the chest with one hand. Give up to 5 firm back blows between the shoulder blades with the heel of your hand.
Step 4: Check after each blow — if the obstruction clears, stop.

Step 5 — ABDOMINAL THRUSTS (Heimlich): Stand behind the person. Place one foot forward for balance. Make a fist with one hand, place it thumb-side against the abdomen, just above the navel and below the breastbone.
Step 6: Grasp your fist with the other hand. Give up to 5 sharp, upward thrusts.
Step 7: Alternate 5 back blows and 5 abdominal thrusts until the object is expelled or the person loses consciousness.
Step 8: If the person becomes unconscious — lower them carefully to the floor, call 112, begin CPR.

Step 9 (INFANT under 1 year): Hold face-down on your forearm, head lower than chest. Give 5 back blows. Turn face-up, give 5 chest thrusts with 2 fingers on center of chest. Do NOT use abdominal thrusts on infants.
Step 10 (PREGNANT or OBESE): Use chest thrusts instead of abdominal thrusts — place hands on center of chest.
warnings: Do NOT perform abdominal thrusts on infants under 1 year.
Do NOT do a blind finger sweep — only remove an object if you can clearly see it.
Do NOT slap a choking person on the back while they are upright and coughing effectively.
Always seek medical attention after abdominal thrusts — internal injury is possible.
when_to_call_emergency: Call 112 if the person cannot cough, speak, or breathe, or if they lose consciousness.
video_search_term: Heimlich maneuver choking adult child Red Cross

title: Spinal Injury
summary: Suspected spinal injuries require extreme care. Incorrect movement can cause permanent paralysis.
steps: Step 1: Call 112 immediately. Do not move the person.
Step 2: Suspect spinal injury if: high-speed collision, fall from height, diving accident, direct blow to head/neck/back, or person complains of neck/back pain, numbness, or tingling.
Step 3: Keep the person completely still — tell them not to move.
Step 4: Stabilize the head and neck in the position found — do not attempt to straighten.
Step 5: Kneel behind the person's head. Place your hands on either side of the head, fingers pointing toward the feet. Hold firmly without moving the head.
Step 6: Maintain this position until emergency services arrive.
Step 7: If the person must be moved (fire, flooding, no pulse) — use the log-roll technique with at least 3 people, keeping head, neck, and spine aligned.
Step 8: If the person is in a vehicle — do not remove them unless there is immediate danger. Wait for emergency services.
Step 9: Monitor breathing. If breathing stops — begin CPR, accepting that some movement is unavoidable.
Step 10: Keep the person warm and reassured. Do not give food or water.
warnings: Do NOT move the person unless there is immediate life-threatening danger.
Do NOT remove a helmet — unless airway cannot be maintained.
Do NOT attempt to straighten the neck or spine.
Do NOT leave the person alone.
when_to_call_emergency: Call 112 immediately for any suspected spinal injury.
video_search_term: spinal injury first aid immobilization technique
